nameMeanings.name: Davis
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Welsh: patronymic meaning ‘Dafydd's (son)’, equivalent to Welsh ap Dafydd, the Welsh form of David . The spelling Davis is more typical in southwestern England northwards as far as Lancashire, where the frequency of the surname largely reflects Welsh migration, but may sometimes represent a native English surname based on Davy (compare Davies ). Davis (including in the sense 2 below) is the eighth most frequent surname in the US. It is also very common among African Americans.
Irish and Scottish: adopted for Gaelic Mac Daibhéid ‘son of David’; see McDevitt . Compare Davies .

History: John Davis or Davys (c. 1550–1605) was an English navigator who searched for the Northwest Passage. — By the 18th century there were numerous persons named Davis in America, including the jurist John Davis, born in 1761 in Plymouth, MA, and Henry Davis, a clergyman and college president, who was born in 1771 in East Hampton, NY. — Jefferson Davis, born in 1808 in KY, was president of the Confederate States of America from 1861 to 1865.

variants.name: David
meanings: Jewish, Welsh, Scottish, English, French, Breton, Portuguese, Czech, Croatian, and Slovenian; Hungarian (Dávid), Slovak (mainly Dávid): from the Hebrew personal name David (in Hungarian and Slovak spelled Dávid), interpreted as ‘beloved’. The name has been perennially popular among Jews, in honor of the Biblical king of this name. His prominence, and the vivid narrative of his life contained in the First Book of Samuel, led to adoption of the name among Christians in the Middle Ages in various parts of Europe. In Britain, the popularity of this as a personal name was increased for two reasons. Firstly by virtue of its being the name of the patron saint of Wales who was abbot-bishop in the 6th century at what became known as Saint David's in Pembrokeshire. There are numerous dedications and placenames honouring the saint in south Wales, and it is no coincidence that the modern surname is heavily concentrated there, especially in Glamorgan. Secondly, the name was borne by two kings of Scotland (David I, reigning 1124–53, and David II, 1329–71). Its popularity in Russia is largely due to the fact that this was the ecclesiastical name adopted by Saint Gleb (died 1015), one of two sons of Prince Vladimir of Kiev who were martyred for their Christian zeal. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from some other languages, especially Assyrian/Chaldean Dawid and Arabic Daud (with variants, such as Daoud and Dawood ).

History: Guillaume David from France married Marie Armand in Trois-Rivières, QC, in 1656. — This surname is listed in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors and also in the similar register of the Huguenot Society of America.

variants.name: Davies
meanings: Welsh and English: variant of Davis , a patronymic equivalent to Welsh ap Dafydd ‘Dafydd's son’. The name is especially numerous in south Wales. This form of the name probably shows the influence of Davy , the common English form of the personal name David in the late medieval and early modern periods (see Davey ).

variants.name: Daves
meanings: Welsh and English: probably a variant of   Davis   or   Davies  .
In some cases also German (Däves): from a shortened form of the personal name Matthäus (see Matthew ). This surname is very rare in Germany.
In some cases probably also an Americanized form of German   Tewes  , a cognate of 2 above.

nameMeanings.name: Augusta
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: feminine form of   Augustus  , which enjoyed a vogue in Britain towards the end of the 19th century but has since fallen out of favour.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

en.lifeSummary: When Augusta Davis was born in 1539, in Buckinghamshire, England, her father, Harford Davis, was 25 and her mother, Rebecca Nesbitt, was 20. She married William Matthew Gibson in 1560, in Buckinghamshire, England. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 4 daughters. She died in 1608, in her hometown, at the age of 69.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Augusta Davis est née en 1539, à Buckinghamshire, Angleterre, son père, Harford Davis, avait 25 ans et sa mère, Rebecca Nesbitt, avait 20 ans. Elle a épousé William Matthew Gibson en 1560, à Buckinghamshire, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 4 filles. Elle est décédée en 1608, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 69 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Augusta Davis 1539 in Buckinghamshire, England geboren wurde, war ihr Vater Harford Davis 25 und ihre Mutter Rebecca Nesbitt 20. Sie hat 1560 in Buckinghamshire, England William Matthew Gibson geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 2 Söhne und 4 Töchter. Sie starb 1608 im Alter von 69 Jahren in ihrem Geburtsort.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Augusta Davis è nata il nel 1539 nel Buckinghamshire, England, sua padre, Harford Davis, aveva 25 anni e sua madre, Rebecca Nesbitt, aveva 20 anni. Lei sposato William Matthew Gibson nel 1560, nel Buckinghamshire, England. Erano genitori di almeno 2 figli e 4 figlie. Morì nel 1608, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 69 anni.
